lowUnverified.title: UP_NEXT_DESC sort comparator does a linear libraryEntries.find() per item inside sortBy
lowUnverified.detail: sortContinueWatchingEntries's new UP_NEXT_DESC branch calls `libraryEntries?.find(e => e.media?.id === n.baseAnime?.id)` inside the per-item sort-key callback, giving O(n·m) library lookups (n = continue-watching count, m = full library size) instead of pre-building a lookup map. Likely negligible at typical list sizes but is an N+1-style pattern.

lowUnverified.title: Loading-screen 'most recent update wins' logic can show stale/wrong text on initial mount
lowUnverified.detail: video-core-loading-screen.tsx uses two separate useEffects (one keyed on debridMsg, one on loadingState) to implement 'whichever channel updated last wins'. Both effects fire unconditionally on the component's first render (mount), and since the loadingState effect is declared second it always runs after and overwrites whatever the debrid effect set — so if the debridstream atom already held a message before this screen mounted (e.g. residual state from a still-open pill), the initial displayed text is loadingState, not necessarily the actually-most-recent value, contradicting the stated recency intent.

lowUnverified.title: Preload path passes context.Background() to findBestTorrent, dropping upstream's cancellation plumbing
lowUnverified.detail: Upstream v3.9.0 added a ctx parameter to findBestTorrent specifically so callers can abort the aggregator search + torrent analysis; the merge resolution adopted the new signature but passed context.Background() in the fork's preload path even though preloadCtx (with its cancel stored in preloadInflight) is used by every other call in the same closure. A cancelled preload (provider change, clearAllPreloadsLocked, shutdown) therefore keeps running the most expensive step to completion, wasting aggregator/debrid API budget; later ctx checks only prevent storing the result. Still present at HEAD.
lowUnverified.evidence: 20596e8b (resolution hunk in internal/debrid/client/stream.go); live at HEAD internal/debrid/client/stream.go:1260 vs preloadCtx used at :1240-1249

lowUnverified.title: PlaybackTypeNoneAndAwait waiter can block forever on hook-gated stream-check failure paths
lowUnverified.detail: startStream's download goroutine omits ready() on two exits inside the OnDebridSkipStreamCheck DefaultPrevented check loop (ctx-cancel and 'Cannot stream the file'), and a recovered panic also skips it, so a noneAndAwait caller blocks on <-readyCh forever (leaked goroutine / hung request). The nakama watch-party host uses noneAndAwait, so a hang there would stall a host stream setup goroutine. Requires a plugin that prevents default on the skip-check hook (or a panic), so it is dormant in this deployment. Same structure exists in upstream origin/main.

lowUnverified.title: cancelStream early-returns before clearing the share triple when RemoveTorrent is set and the provider is unset
lowUnverified.detail: The fork fixed the prior-audit 'torn share state' item by clearing url/itemId/fileId together at the end of cancelStream, but the RemoveTorrent branch returns early when GetProvider() fails (provider disabled mid-stream), skipping the clear. shareSnapshot can then hand a stale torrentItemId/fileId with an empty URL to the watch-room join path. Narrow window (explicit remove-cancel while debrid settings are being disabled). Residual sliver of the fable-audit §9.6 item, not a regression of the 800f59bd fix.

lowUnverified.title: Preload eviction paths delete entries without releasing their prewarmed MKV metadata
lowUnverified.detail: evictIfNeededLocked (TTL sweep + speculative-budget eviction) and clearAllPreloadsLocked delete preload entries without calling DropStreamMetadata, unlike CleanupWatchedPrewarms and the episode-end paths which do. A metadata-warmed entry whose selection rolls over (3h airing TTL) leaves its old URL's parser (font attachments, tens of MB) in RAM until the directstream parser cache's own 2h TTL reclaims it. Bounded and self-healing, but avoidable RAM pressure on the 8GB Pi during multi-user prewarm.

lowUnverified.title: persistPrewarm UserTags merge is a non-transactional read-merge-write; concurrent writers can drop a stakeholder tag
lowUnverified.detail: persistPrewarm reads the existing row's UserTags, merges the acting user, and upserts the whole row with no lock or transaction. Two users on the same account persisting the same media/episode/profile row concurrently (prewarm drain + play-time URL refresh) can lose one user's tag. The n-2 CleanupWatchedPrewarms of the surviving tagged user then deletes the row a slower user still needs — their badge/DB reuse disappears until the next prewarm tick re-persists it. Self-healing within one 10-min tick, so impact is cosmetic.

lowUnverified.title: Prewarm drains from successive ticks can overlap, weakening the one-resolve-at-a-time guarantee
lowUnverified.detail: PrewarmStreams spawns one drain goroutine per 10-minute tick with no in-progress guard; if a drain outlives the tick (many users x 6 targets with slow cold resolves can approach 10 min), the next tick's drain runs concurrently and blocking resolves overlap — the exact overlap c285a4fb's serialization was meant to remove. Damage is capped: the shared prewarmLimiter still paces kickoffs at 1/1.5s across all drains, per-key inflight/fresh checks dedupe targets, and requestdlLimiter bounds TorBox calls. Typical ticks are cheap (fresh-key skips), so this only bites pathological cold-start ticks.

lowUnverified.title: Repository.settings is replaced without synchronization while background prewarm goroutines read it
lowUnverified.detail: InitializeProvider assigns r.settings with no mutex while the prewarm drain, accountHash, GetPrewarmStatus, and preloadStreamWith read it from other goroutines — a data race per the Go memory model (race detector would flag it), though practically benign as a single aligned pointer swap. Pre-fork upstream pattern; the fork's prewarm layer added most of the concurrent readers. An atomic.Pointer or copy-on-read via GetSettings would silence it.

lowUnverified.title: TestValidateReleaseUrl permanently red: fork pinned allowedGitHubOwners to ClinShaiju but test still expects 5rahim URLs to pass
lowUnverified.detail: Fork commit c38ae2bd changed allowedGitHubOwners to []string{"ClinShaiju"} (deliberate — Pi auto-update pulls from the fork's releases) but never updated the upstream test, which asserts https://github.com/5rahim/seanime/releases/... validates true. 'go test ./internal/util/' fails on every run (version_test.go:301), masking real regressions in the package. Fix is test-only: update or fork-annotate the 5rahim expectation.

lowUnverified.title: ResolveArchiveEntryPath false 'escapes destination' for Unix-style destRoot on Windows — TestResolveArchiveEntryPath fails on the dev machine
lowUnverified.detail: normalizeComparablePath routes destRoot "/tmp/extracted" (no backslashes) through the filepath.Abs branch (-> "h:/tmp/extracted" on Windows) but the joined child — which gains backslashes via filepath.Join/FromSlash — through the looksLikeWindowsPath branch WITHOUT Abs (-> "/tmp/extracted/folder/file.txt"), so the descendant prefix check fails and a legitimate nested entry is rejected as traversal. Upstream code (5rahim 'security overhaul' 5d885ae5 / 45be2925, arrived via v3.9 merges); zero production impact since real update-extraction paths are OS-consistent, but it permanently breaks 'go test ./internal/util/' on Windows (fs_test.go:118).
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/util/fs.go:114-134 (normalizeComparablePath branch divergence), fs.go:147-170, internal/util/fs_test.go:118; commits 5d885ae5, 45be2925 (upstream)

lowUnverified.title: buildCandidates runs CleanReleaseName twice per torrent and re-runs habari.Parse per ranking pass despite an existing per-name parse cache
lowUnverified.detail: buildCandidates calls util.CleanReleaseName(t.Name) once for habari.Parse and again for lowerName (3 regex passes each), and re-parses every candidate with habari on every filterAndSort/Rank invocation. torrent/search.go already maintains a package-level metadataCache keyed by name (SearchData.TorrentMetadata) holding the identical CleanReleaseName+habari.Parse result, but autoselect never reuses it. Cost is tens of ms per ranking pass over a few hundred aggregator results on the Pi — Rank runs on every manual-search render and autoselect on every play. Minor; worth folding into the next autoselect touch, not urgent.

lowUnverified.title: updateCompletedProgress's stream.(*BaseStream) assertion can never succeed — the directstream-level completion progress path is dead code
lowUnverified.detail: Current streams are always concrete types (*DebridStream, *TorrentStream, *LocalFileStream, *UrlStream, *Nakama) that EMBED BaseStream, so the assertion at stream.go:594 always fails and the platform UpdateEntryProgress call there never runs. Harmless today because the live auto-progress path is the mediacore Coordinator's SetupSharedEffects CompletedEvent handler (mediacore.go:495-530, with AutoUpdateProgress + already-watched guards), but the dead branch (and its unused updateProgress sync.Once) masks any future regression in that coordinator path. Upstream code from 8b5c6bba.
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/directstream/stream.go:594-600; live path internal/mediacore/mediacore.go:495-530; origin commit 8b5c6bba

lowUnverified.title: Manager.animeCache is never initialized; getAnime would nil-panic on first use (currently unreferenced)
lowUnverified.detail: NewManager initializes parserCache and streamInfoCache but never animeCache (manager.go:79); getAnime dereferences it at manager.go:402/423 and result.Cache methods are not nil-receiver safe. getAnime has no callers in the package (unexported), so this is dead code with a latent nil-deref should anyone wire it up. Upstream origin (02ee40df).

lowUnverified.title: CloseOpen reads m.preparingTarget after releasing playbackMu (data race, stale-target AbortOpen possible)
lowUnverified.detail: stream.go:185 evaluates m.preparingTarget as an argument to m.abortOpen AFTER playbackMu.Unlock(); a concurrent BeginOpenWithTarget can mutate or clear it, so the abort can be signaled at a stale/empty target and the read is a -race-detectable data race. Practical impact is a mis-routed abort notification at worst. Upstream code (0457d846).

lowUnverified.title: Content-type HEAD/GET probe bypasses the per-token CDN gate, never retries transient 429s, and latches failure per stream instance
lowUnverified.detail: getContentTypeAndLengthWithHeaders (stream.go:817-922) hits the debrid link outside cdnTokenGateInst (adding to the exact stream-start burst the fork gate was built to prevent) and the GET fallback never checks resp.StatusCode — a throttled probe yields an error-page content type, FetchStreamInfoWithHeaders rejects it (uncached, so no poisoning), and contentTypeOnce latches the empty result for that stream object, failing the play with 'failed to load content type' instead of retrying with backoff like the serve/warm paths do. Prewarm's cached HEAD (streamInfoCache, 2h TTL) hides this on the binge path; a user retry creates a new stream and recovers.
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/directstream/stream.go:817-922 (esp. 877 unchecked GET status); internal/directstream/httpstream.go:218-241 (contentTypeOnce latch); gate rationale in internal/directstream/cdngate.go:12-17

lowUnverified.title: CleanupExpiredSessions is defined but never called
lowUnverified.detail: db.CleanupExpiredSessions() (bulk delete of expired session rows) has no caller anywhere in the tree. Expired/long-lived session rows are only removed lazily when their exact token is presented again (GetSessionUser deletes on access), so with a 30-day TTL, revoked and stale sessions accumulate in sqlite indefinitely. Minor hygiene/growth issue; wire it into startup or the periodic cleanup manager.
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/database/db/session.go:54-56 (definition); grep shows no non-definition callers of CleanupExpiredSessions

lowUnverified.title: Access-log enricher re-resolves the session on every logged request
lowUnverified.detail: The lecho Enricher calls RequestUsername -> Database.GetSessionUser(token) (a DB query, plus GetAdminUser on password-less installs) for every non-skipped request purely to tag the log line, duplicating the identical GetSessionUser that IdentityMiddleware performs microseconds later. Each logged API request therefore resolves the same session token against sqlite twice. High-frequency endpoints are excluded via urisToSkip so the blast radius is bounded, but the duplication is avoidable (resolve once, stash on the request).

lowUnverified.title: User-login throttle is username-keyed (account-lockout DoS) with an unbounded map
lowUnverified.detail: loginThrottleRecord keys failures/lockedAt by the attacker-supplied username, so anyone past the server-password gate can lock a known account (e.g. admin) out for loginLockout by sending loginMaxFailures bad passwords, and can grow the maps without bound by cycling distinct usernames (entries are only pruned on that username's own next attempt or success). Both require the shared server password, so impact is limited, but IP-keying or a global cap would be safer.
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/handlers/user_auth.go loginThrottleRecord/loginThrottleCheck (adjacent to scope; auth layer)

lowUnverified.title: JoinRoom racing a host LeaveRoom can strand the joiner in a deleted room
lowUnverified.detail: LeaveRoom's host path collects the notify list under room.mu, unlocks, then deletes the room from h.rooms; a concurrent JoinRoom that already resolved the room pointer via getRoom can complete its membership write after the delete. The joiner receives a room state and sets currentWatchRoomAtom, but the room no longer exists in the hub: no NakamaWatchRoomClosed is ever sent to them (the closed event targeted only the pre-collected members) and the reaper cannot see the orphaned object. The client sits in a ghost room until a manual leave (which returns nil idempotently). Narrow window, self-recoverable.

lowUnverified.title: MpvCore auto-skip ignores Intro/Outro-labeled and unlabeled ~90s chapters — fork fix 19bed7eb applies to VideoCore only
lowUnverified.detail: The upstream opEdChapters memo added to mpv-core-player-inner.tsx by the ce4181ac merge only accepts chapters whose vc_getChapterType is exactly 'Opening'/'Ending', then falls back to AniSkip. The fork's vc_getOPEDChapters (19bed7eb: Intro/Outro with a 60-150s sanity window, plus the ~90s positional heuristic for generically-labeled chapters) is consumed only by video-core.tsx/video-core-time-range.tsx. On Denshi MpvCore — the primary player — releases whose chapters are labeled Intro/Outro or unlabeled don't auto-skip unless AniSkip has data. Not a regression (MpvCore never had the fork logic), but the fork's fix silently stopped covering the daily-driver path when playback moved to MpvCore.

lowUnverified.title: vc_loadingMediaIdAtom is never cleared on the MpvCore path, so stale artwork can win over the real media
lowUnverified.detail: The loading screen prefers the atom over the actual playbackInfo media (`loadingMediaId || media?.id`). native-player.tsx clears the atom on abort-open and terminate, but nothing under _features/mpv-core ever clears it. After a debrid start sets it, any subsequent MpvCore playback that begins without going through the three setter hooks (playlist/auto-next pushed by the server, watch-room join) shows the previous show's fanart/logo on the loading screen. Fix: clear the atom in MpvCore's terminate/ended path or invert the precedence to `media?.id || loadingMediaId`.
lowUnverified.evidence: seanime-web/src/app/(main)/_features/video-core/video-core-loading-screen.tsx:47-48; setters handle-debrid-stream.ts:106, handle-torrent-stream.ts:57, handle-play-media.ts:48; clears only in native-player.tsx:140,212; zero references under seanime-web/src/app/(main)/_features/mpv-core/

lowUnverified.title: PlaybackPlayPill calls hooks after a conditional early return and subscribes the always-mounted floating pill to per-tick currentTime atoms
lowUnverified.detail: In playback-play-pill.tsx the `if (isNativePlayerComponent)` block returns before useAtomValue(vc_globalMiniPlayerAtom)/useAtomValue(mc_currentTime)/useAtomValue(vc_currentTime)/a useEffect/useMemo run — a rules-of-hooks violation that stays latent only because the prop is static per mount site; if it ever became dynamic React would throw. Separately, the floating instance mounted globally in main-layout.tsx subscribes to mc_currentTime and vc_currentTime, so it re-renders on every playback position tick for the whole session even while it renders null. Upstream code arrived via the 20596e8b merge; the fork's ce4181ac merge also carried changes into this file, so it is fork-maintained surface.

lowUnverified.title: Upstream mediacore persists resume position to disk on every 1 Hz StatusEvent with no throttle
lowUnverified.detail: Upstream commit 8b5c6bba wires every player.StatusEvent (emitted at 1/s by the web player interval) straight into continuityManager.UpdateWatchHistoryItem, i.e. a full JSON rewrite of the resume bucket file every second of playback, independent of the fork's _lw amplification. The fork's own playbackmanager live-save path deliberately throttles the identical persist to 10 s (af16ba17, saveLiveProgressThrottled), so the streaming path lacks the throttle the fork already established as correct. A ~10 s time.Since gate in updateContinuityState would cut per-second disk writes ~10x while keeping pause/seek/stop saves exact (those flow through PausedEvent/SeekedEvent/stop paths).
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/mediacore/mediacore.go:485-490 (StatusEvent -> updateContinuityState, upstream 8b5c6bba); contrast internal/library/playbackmanager/progress_tracking.go:216-234 (fork's 10s throttle, af16ba17)

lowUnverified.title: franchise-group cache stores the full group JSON once per member id and never sweeps expired entries
lowUnverified.detail: CacheFranchiseGroup writes the entire FranchiseGroup (each member's BaseAnime serialized twice, via Seasons/Extras plus WatchOrder) under every member id, so a k-member franchise stores ~2k full-media copies and performs k sequential full-file rewrites of franchise-group.cache. filecache only deletes an expired entry when that exact key is re-read (Get) and no Range/sweep ever runs on this bucket, so 24h-expired groups for entries never revisited persist indefinitely on disk and in the always-resident in-memory store map. Same full-rewrite-per-Set pattern applies to ResolveMany filling franchise-ref on a cold library collapse (one full-file rewrite per resolved id). Growth is bounded by franchises ever viewed, so impact is gradual RAM/disk bloat plus rewrite cost on the Pi, not breakage.

lowUnverified.title: Concurrent uncached franchise requests each run a full AniList relation walk (no singleflight)
lowUnverified.detail: resolveFranchiseGroup checks the 24h group cache then runs FetchMediaTreeAll with a fresh 20-req/s limiter per request; two concurrent requests for the same cold franchise (two clients, or entry page + merged-season race) each execute the full multi-call relation walk against the shared admin AniList client. The per-member-id caching makes steady state cheap, so this only bites on cold/expired bursts, but a singleflight keyed on root media id would remove the duplicate walks. Also note all users' walks consume the admin token's rate budget (shared-infra by design).

lowUnverified.title: getWatchHistory's async delete of a >=90%-complete item can race the immediately-following Set and drop a fresh resume point
lowUnverified.detail: When UpdateWatchHistoryItem finds the stored item at ratio >= 0.9 (e.g. first tick of the next episode after finishing the previous one), getWatchHistory spawns `go Delete(mediaId)` and reports not-found; the caller then Sets a brand-new item under the same key, and the unsynchronized goroutine's Delete can execute after the Set, erasing the just-written resume position. The 1 Hz mediacore tick recreates it within a second, but on the 10 s-throttled playbackmanager path or the sparse onlinestream PATCH path the resume point can stay missing until the next save, and is lost if the player closes in that window. Upstream code (92ad38ae); the fork's durable _lw mirror is unaffected (delete targets only the resume bucket). Fix shape: delete synchronously under m.mu (Delete is cheap) or skip the delete when the caller is about to overwrite.
lowUnverified.evidence: internal/continuity/history.go:433-446 (go func Delete inside getWatchHistory) vs :203-206 (subsequent Set in UpdateWatchHistoryItem); upstream 92ad38ae

nonLive.title: Preload feature adds concurrent writers to StreamManager fields with no mutex at introduction time
nonLive.detail: 37d5a2ab's playPreloadedStream() writes s.currentTorrentItemId, s.currentStreamUrl, s.downloadCtxCancelFunc, s.playbackSubscriberCtxCancelFunc directly (mirroring startStream), while only the new preloadedStream/preloadCancelFunc fields got a dedicated preloadMu. At this point in history none of those shared scalars were guarded by any lock, so a preload-consuming request racing a fresh startStream (or CancelStream) could interleave writes/reads of the same fields from different goroutines. GetStreamURL() in repository.go also reads currentStreamUrl unguarded.
nonLive.evidence: internal/debrid/client/stream.go (37d5a2ab): playPreloadedStream sets s.currentTorrentItemId/s.currentStreamUrl/s.downloadCtxCancelFunc without any lock; internal/debrid/client/repository.go:308 GetStreamURL() reads streamManager.currentStreamUrl unguarded

nonLive.title: Same-day autoselect language-tier scoring shipped with successive bugs, each patched by a later same-batch commit
nonLive.detail: f10af77d's language-demotion logic matched against c.lowerName (raw name, size tokens included), which was only fixed to use the size/emoji-stripped name three commits later in 4fd80519 (the '2.32 GB' -> 'gb' -> English false match). Similarly the 'dual-audio implies English' heuristic added in 7ed57855 was replaced two commits later in 30df6a48 because it mis-ranked dual/fr releases. Between commits in this same batch, auto-select ranking would have misranked non-English aggregator releases as English for real users (this deployment auto-updates the running binary only on release, not per-commit, but the window is worth noting for audit completeness).
nonLive.evidence: internal/torrents/autoselect/comparison.go: f10af77d (2026-06-19 20:45) vs 4fd80519 (2026-06-19 22:47); 7ed57855 (21:42) vs 30df6a48 (22:41)

nonLive.title: Preview-list reordering keys a rank map by InfoHash, which a00983fe-era search.go still fills with the raw torrent Name for hash-less aggregator results
nonLive.detail: HandleSearchTorrent's SortByAutoSelect branch builds `rank[t.InfoHash] = i` from the ordered list then looks up each preview by its torrent's InfoHash; at a00983fe's time (and still today) torrent/search.go falls back to `t.InfoHash = t.Name` when the field is empty, so two aggregator results that happen to share the same release name would collide in the map and all but one would silently take the last one's rank instead of their own, only affecting cosmetic ordering of the preview list, not the auto-selected file.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/torrent_search.go (a00983fe): rank := make(map[string]int...); rank[t.InfoHash] = i; internal/torrents/torrent/search.go:308-313 t.InfoHash = t.Name fallback (current code uses t.Identity() instead, but was t.Name at a00983fe's time)

nonLive.title: a00983fe and 166e2960 rewrote several Go/TSX files with CRLF line endings, producing full-file diffs that obscure the real logic change
nonLive.detail: Files such as internal/torrents/autoselect/comparison.go, finder.go, file_selection.go, analyzer.go, torrent_search.go and multiple torrent-search *.tsx files were re-saved with CRLF instead of the repo's LF, so `git show --stat` reports hundreds-to-thousands of changed lines for edits that were actually a few lines; this makes review/blame harder and was corrected the next day by a dedicated normalization commit.
nonLive.evidence: e.g. internal/torrents/autoselect/autoselect.go changed 0a->0d0a between a00983fe~1 and a00983fe (confirmed via xxd); fixed by 01400140 'chore: normalize line endings to LF' (2026-06-20)

nonLive.title: aa346c98 mutates the shared *hibiketorrent.AnimeTorrent pointer's InfoHash in place during file selection
nonLive.detail: selectFileFromDebrid overwrites t.InfoHash (and earlier t.MagnetLink) on the same *AnimeTorrent pointer that came from the ranked/filtered candidate list, rather than operating on a local copy; within a single auto-select call this is harmless since the list is exclusively owned by that request, but the pattern of mutating shared torrent objects in place (also used for MagnetLink) makes future reuse of the same slice across concurrent operations easy to get wrong.
nonLive.evidence: internal/torrents/autoselect/file_selection.go (aa346c98): `t.InfoHash = h` after `t.MagnetLink = magnet`, on the same pointer passed in from the caller's candidate slice

nonLive.title: smartCachedPrioritization now makes cache status strictly dominate format quality within an audio/episode tier, for both auto-select and the manual 'Auto' sort
nonLive.detail: 70f34f49 replaces the old '70%-of-top-score' guard (which kept low-quality cached torrents from beating good uncached ones) with an unconditional cache-first sort inside a band: 'cached releases come first (cache outranks format)... a cached stream plays instantly, so it always wins'. Rank() (which backs smartCachedPrioritization) is used for both AutoSelect.filter and RankTorrentsForDisplay (the manual torrent-search 'Auto' sort), so a much lower-quality cached release can now outrank a higher-quality uncached one in the same audio tier in both flows. This directly reorders candidates by cache status, which is the pattern user memory later frames as something latency work must never do ('quality ranking is source of truth').
nonLive.evidence: internal/torrents/autoselect/comparison.go:654-720 (smartCachedPrioritization, scoreBand); internal/debrid/client/finder.go:220 (RankTorrentsForDisplay -> r.autoSelect.Rank); 38dc857f (2026-06-23, later commit) adds within-band quality tiebreakers but does not touch smartCachedPrioritization/scoreBand, so this behavior is unchanged at HEAD

nonLive.title: torrent/search.go reuses the InfoHash field to store a non-hash Identity() value (StreamUrl or Name) when InfoHash is empty
nonLive.detail: 8b3c1b1b changes `t.InfoHash = t.Name` (already a smell) to `t.InfoHash = t.Identity()`, i.e. still writing a non-hash string (a URL, in the new StreamUrl case) into a field named/typed as an info-hash. Existing call sites are careful to check `StreamUrl != ""` before treating InfoHash as a real hash (stream.go, file_selection.go), and a pre-existing fix (aa346c98) already re-derives the real btih from the magnet for debrid lookups, so no live path currently misuses it — but any new code that does `if t.InfoHash != "" { client.GetTorrentInfo(...) }` without checking StreamUrl first would silently break on these synthetic values.
nonLive.evidence: internal/torrents/torrent/search.go:305-315; internal/extension/hibike/torrent/types.go:190-201 (Identity()); internal/torrents/autoselect/file_selection.go:259-265 (pre-existing stale comment still says 'fills t.InfoHash with the torrent NAME')

nonLive.title: AniList 'PARENT' relation is unconditionally treated as a franchise extra, which could misclassify a genuine parent season if it's reached only via that edge
nonLive.detail: Both `extraRelationTypes` (handlers/anime_franchise.go) and `sideStoryRelations` (library/anime/franchise.go isExtra) list MediaRelationParent alongside SIDE_STORY/SPIN_OFF/SUMMARY, so any member reached only via a PARENT edge (rather than via the SEQUEL/PREQUEL tree walk) is always classified IsExtra=true and tagged, even if it is TV-format and would otherwise be a mainline season. Whether this scenario actually occurs depends on AniList's relation-graph conventions, which weren't verified in this batch.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/anime_franchise.go:35-40; internal/library/anime/franchise.go (sideStoryRelations map, isExtra)

nonLive.title: Client-initiated preload requests always get PrewarmMetadata=true, including speculative hover prewarms
nonLive.detail: HandleDebridStartStream sets `PrewarmMetadata: b.Preload` for every client-issued preload (internal/handlers/debrid.go), so both the high-certainty in-playback next-episode preload and the speculative 700ms-debounced episode-hover prewarm (use-debrid-prewarm.ts, capped at 8 speculative slots) trigger full MKV metadata parsing plus a CDN 'warm' GET of up to 48MB (manager.go computeWarmBytes). This contradicts the explicit design comments in manager.go/prewarm.go/use-debrid-prewarm.ts stating metadata prewarm should be reserved for 'high-certainty targets... not every speculative prewarm' since fonts load tens of MB into RAM. This matches the exact 'prewarm font fan-out' 429 contributor already documented in fable-audit.md's appendix.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/debrid.go:411-414 (c38ae2bd); internal/directstream/manager.go PrewarmStreamMetadata doc comment; internal/debrid/client/stream.go PrewarmMetadata field comment; fable-audit.md:435

nonLive.title: A higher-priority preload request that arrives while a lower-priority resolve for the same key is in flight doesn't get upgraded to priority
nonLive.detail: preloadStream's in-flight dedup (`if _, inflight := s.preloadInflight[key]; inflight { return nil }`) silently skips scheduling when any resolve for the key is already running, regardless of the new request's Priority. The already-running goroutine's captured `opts.Priority` (from when it started) is what gets stored on completion, so a hover-triggered speculative resolve that's still in flight when the continue-watching prewarm targets the same episode finishes as non-priority and is subject to the 8-slot speculative eviction budget instead of being protected.
nonLive.evidence: internal/debrid/client/stream.go preloadStream() and evictIfNeededLocked() (c38ae2bd)

nonLive.title: dataUserID fell back to the admin user for ANY unresolved identity, not just password-less installs, when introduced
nonLive.detail: As introduced in a89fd47a, `dataUserID(c)` returns the admin's ID whenever `CurrentUserID(c) == 0`, with no check on whether a server password is configured. On a networked (password-protected) multi-user server, a request that only knows the shared server password but has no user session (IdentityMiddleware case 3, 96f8ab41) resolves to `CurrentUserID==0` and this fallback then silently reads/writes the ADMIN's theme (a89fd47a) and playlists (5afca8ea) instead of being denied or scoped anonymously. The later commit e9be7e61 rewrote dataUserID to only fall back to admin when `Config.Server.Password == ""`, explicitly citing 'knowing the shared server password is not the same as being the admin' — confirming this was a real gap during this batch's window.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/identity.go dataUserID() as added in a89fd47a; internal/database/db/theme.go, internal/database/db_bridge/playlist.go (5afca8ea); fixed by e9be7e61

nonLive.title: UpsertTheme's per-user row resolution is check-then-act with no unique constraint backstop, a regression from the prior atomic ON CONFLICT upsert
nonLive.detail: Before a89fd47a, UpsertTheme used `clause.OnConflict{Columns:[id], UpdateAll:true}` against a fixed single row (id=1) — atomic at the DB level. The rewrite instead does `Where(user_id=?).Find(&existing)` then sets `settings.ID` accordingly before `Save`, a non-atomic find-then-decide-insert-or-update sequence. The `Theme.UserID` column is only a plain index (`gorm:"column:user_id;index"`), not unique, so two concurrent theme-save requests for the same user (e.g. rapid color-picker changes, or two tabs) could each see no existing row and both INSERT, leaving two Theme rows per user; GetTheme's `Limit(1).Find` would then non-deterministically return either.
nonLive.evidence: internal/database/db/theme.go UpsertTheme (a89fd47a); internal/database/models/models.go Theme.UserID (`gorm:"column:user_id;index"`, no uniqueIndex)

nonLive.title: Auto-generated admin one-time password is logged in plaintext via logger.Warn
nonLive.detail: bootstrapAdminUser logs the freshly-generated admin password (`util.GenerateCryptoID()`) at Warn level on first install (`Created initial admin user %q with password: %s`), and also logs the operator-supplied password when set via --admin-password. This is a deliberate bootstrap/recovery UX (the manual test checklist explicitly instructs grepping journalctl for it), but it does mean the plaintext admin credential persists in server logs/journald for as long as log retention allows.
nonLive.evidence: internal/core/modules.go bootstrapAdminUser() (382318fa); profile-support-testing.md 'Generated admin password' line

nonLive.title: HandleUserLogin has no brute-force throttle; HandleUserRegister accepts an unvalidated role string
nonLive.detail: At introduction (96f8ab41), HandleUserLogin performs bcrypt verification with no per-IP/username rate limiting, and HandleUserRegister passes the client-supplied `role` string straight to CreateUser (which does clamp any non-'admin' string to 'user', so this can't self-escalate, but an admin typo silently creates the wrong role class without a validation error). Both are already tracked in fable-audit.md and fixed by 8760d144 (5-failure/30s lockout, role whitelist).
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/user_auth.go HandleUserLogin, HandleUserRegister (96f8ab41); fable-audit.md lines 342-354; fixed by 8760d144

nonLive.title: getTorrentsCached's cached slice is returned by reference and could alias a slice still being appended to elsewhere
nonLive.detail: getTorrentsCached stores and returns `t.mylist` (a `[]*Torrent`) directly rather than a copy; if any other code path appends to a returned slice while another caller iterates a concurrently-cached copy sharing the same backing array (both referencing `t.mylist`), that's a narrow-window backing-array data race. This mirrors a pattern already flagged for a related area in fork-audit.md ('TorBox slice-append race'); not confirmed to be hit in this exact code path but the sharing-by-reference is present here too.
nonLive.evidence: internal/debrid/torbox/torbox.go getTorrentsCached() (c38ae2bd)

nonLive.title: In-place merged-season playback always prefers debrid auto-select over an existing local library file
nonLive.detail: playEpisode (merged-season-section.tsx) checks `debridSettings.enabled && streamAutoSelect && ep.aniDBEpisode` first and only falls back to `ep.localFile?.path` if that's not satisfied, so an episode that exists both in the local library and is debrid-auto-selectable always plays via debrid inside the merged view. Whether this matches the ordering used by the equivalent single-cour entry page could not be confirmed from this diff alone.
nonLive.evidence: seanime-web/src/app/(main)/entry/_components/merged-season-section.tsx playEpisode() (d4024486)

nonLive.title: Per-user debrid API key stored in plaintext inside a JSON blob column
nonLive.detail: UserOverrides.DebridApiKey is marshalled unencrypted into UserSettings.Value (a []byte JSON blob) via UpsertUserOverrides/HandleSaveUserDebrid. This mirrors the existing (pre-batch) plaintext storage of the shared server DebridSettings.ApiKey, so it's consistent with the existing pattern rather than a new regression, but it multiplies the number of plaintext third-party API keys at rest in the sqlite DB as more users bring their own debrid.
nonLive.evidence: internal/database/models/user_settings.go:36-40 (commit 82308c5b); internal/handlers/user_auth.go HandleSaveUserDebrid (commit 0dac063a)

nonLive.title: New unlinked non-admin session initially fell back to the admin's shared simulated/local collection
nonLive.detail: As introduced in 526843bb, buildUserSession() for a user who hadn't linked AniList built a simulated_platform.NewSimulatedPlatform backed by the shared a.LocalManager (the admin's offline snapshot), so an unlinked regular user would see and could even write back to the admin's library/lists/continue-watching — a cross-user data leak/corruption path. This was corrected in the same commit batch by 2a844337 (unauthenticated AniList platform + emptyAnimeCollection()/emptyMangaCollection() until the user links their own account), so the window only existed within this batch's own history, not in any released version.
nonLive.evidence: internal/core/session.go (526843bb diff, buildUserSession using simulated_platform.NewSimulatedPlatform); fixed by 2a844337 internal/core/session.go:117-141,242-304

nonLive.title: Single settings Form object is submitted for both admin and non-admin roles, relying on react-hook-form retaining default values for fields whose tab/section is hidden
nonLive.detail: page.tsx's onSubmit always builds the same full payload object (including groupSeasons/autoPlayNextEpisode/etc.) from `data`, and dispatches it to either saveSettings (admin) or saveUserSettings (non-admin, which extracts only overridable fields server-side). Correctness for a non-admin depends on RHF keeping default-value state for controls whose parent tab is conditionally unmounted (isAdmin && <TabsTrigger>) rather than that data being unregistered/reset to a Zod default — this wasn't verified in the diff and is a fragile implicit dependency rather than an explicit contract.
nonLive.evidence: seanime-web/src/app/(main)/settings/page.tsx:353-395 (onSubmit) + tab gating at :214-320, introduced across d4917d8e/ce464c6f/c313d15a

nonLive.title: User-facing "Use server debrid" toggle + own API key form ships with no functional effect yet, and the UI gives no in-context indication of this
nonLive.detail: 0dac063a exposes a full Debrid tab to regular users (toggle off server debrid, enter own provider+API key, Save → 'Debrid settings saved' toast) but the commit message states streaming still always goes through the server debrid; the frontend form itself carries no disclaimer, so a user who fills in their own key and toggles off server debrid gets a success toast while nothing changes about how their streams are served. This was later wired up in a subsequent commit outside this batch (dd6e63a0, per the fork commit list), so the gap only existed for one batch/commit window.
nonLive.evidence: seanime-web/src/app/(main)/settings/_containers/user-debrid-settings.tsx (new file, commit 0dac063a); functional wiring lands later in dd6e63a0 (outside this batch)

nonLive.title: dd6e63a0 claims per-user simultaneous debrid streaming, but the core StreamManager stays a single shared instance
nonLive.detail: dd6e63a0 routes DirectStream/PlaybackManager delivery per-user via SessionModulesFunc, but the actual torrent-selection/download state (currentStreamUrl, currentFileId, downloadCtxCancelFunc, previousStreamOptions, preloads map, lastConsumedKey) lives on one StreamManager per Repository (a process-wide singleton), so two users starting debrid streams concurrently still contend over the same fields. The code comment at stream.go itself (post-fix) documents this exact problem being solved elsewhere. Confirmed fixed later by commit 9b969cc3 'per-user StreamManager + per-user prewarm (concurrent stream isolation)', which is outside this batch.
nonLive.evidence: internal/debrid/client/stream.go:26-70 (struct StreamManager fields, comment on stateMu); commit dd6e63a0; fixed by 9b969cc3 (not in batch 5)

nonLive.title: HandleDebridCancelStream has no per-user guard and cancelStream resolves the wrong user's directStream via shared state
nonLive.detail: As of the end of this batch (5ca1e85f), HandleDebridCancelStream has neither guardStreamingUser nor h.UserOnly, so an anonymous (password-only) client can cancel the currently active debrid stream. Internally, StreamManager.cancelStream resolves which DirectStream manager to close via the single shared previousStreamOptions field (dd6e63a0), so with two users streaming, a cancel from either resolves whichever stream was started LAST server-wide, not the caller's own. This is the same root cause as the StreamManager-singleton finding and is fixed by the same later commit.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/debrid.go (HandleDebridCancelStream, as of 5ca1e85f) — no guard; internal/debrid/client/stream.go cancelStream (dd6e63a0) uses s.repository.previousStreamOptions.Get(); fixed by 9b969cc3 (adds Options.UserID scoping) — not in batch 5

nonLive.title: 2b602d6f's UserOnly gate distinguishes anon vs logged-in but not user vs. other-user for shared debrid torrent management
nonLive.detail: 2b602d6f adds h.UserOnly to /debrid/torrents, /debrid/torrents/download, /debrid/torrents/cancel, /debrid/torrent (DELETE), which blocks anonymous callers, but HandleDebridGetTorrents/HandleDebridDeleteTorrent/HandleDebridCancelDownload call the shared Repository/provider directly with no per-user ownership filter. Any two authenticated non-admin users on the same server can list, cancel, or delete each other's torrents on the shared debrid account. This may be inherent to using one shared debrid account, but the fix's stated goal (isolating multi-user streaming) doesn't cover it.
nonLive.evidence: internal/handlers/debrid.go:247-330 (HandleDebridCancelDownload, HandleDebridDeleteTorrent, HandleDebridGetTorrents — no user filter); internal/handlers/routes.go:577-583 (h.UserOnly added by 2b602d6f)

nonLive.title: 7c87a05b/9cc9aea5's OwnerScopedWSEventManager uses one global atomic 'owner', misrouting events if two streams start concurrently
nonLive.detail: The interim OwnerScopedWSEventManager (7c87a05b, refined 9cc9aea5) has a single atomic owner field for ALL shared streaming/playback modules; SetStreamOwner from one user's stream-start silently reassigns where every other in-flight stream's broadcast/targeted events go. This is explicitly documented in-code as a known 'ponytail' limitation for the single-active-stream assumption, and is superseded two commits later in the same batch by 3b45a2e7's per-session ScopedWSEventManager (fixed at construction, no shared mutable owner).
nonLive.evidence: internal/events/scoped.go (7c87a05b: OwnerScopedWSEventManager with atomic.Uint64 owner); superseded by 3b45a2e7 in the same batch

nonLive.title: 3b45a2e7 is a self-declared WIP commit that (temporarily) leaves non-admin streams unrouted to per-session modules
nonLive.detail: 3b45a2e7's commit message states plainly 'handlers + debrid/torrentstream still call the App globals, so non-admin streams are not yet routed to their session modules. NOT deployable until that wiring lands in the following commits.' This is intentional mid-refactor scaffolding, immediately completed by the very next commit (dd6e63a0) in the same batch, so it never reached a deployable/released state in this form.
nonLive.evidence: commit 3b45a2e7 message; internal/handlers/debrid.go, directstream.go, mediastream.go, playback_manager.go, torrentstream.go (SetStreamOwner calls removed, not yet replaced); fixed by dd6e63a0 (same batch)

nonLive.title: 76afee6b introduces a config-gated anon-hardening default (defaults false), e9be7e61 makes it unconditional the same day
nonLive.detail: 76afee6b adds server.RequireUserLogin defaulting to false specifically 'to preserve current behavior' until Denshi is rebuilt with login support, meaning the admin-data-leak-to-anon fix was NOT actually active by default when merged. e9be7e61, committed later the same day, removes the flag and makes the hardening unconditional. Net effect is correct by end of day, but there was a window where the documented security fix required an explicit config change that most deployments would not have made.
nonLive.evidence: internal/core/config.go / internal/core/session.go (76afee6b: RequireUserLogin bool defaulting false); internal/core/config.go / internal/core/session.go (e9be7e61: flag removed, hardening unconditional)

nonLive.title: broadcastRoomState marshals the live *WatchRoom (with its live Participants map) outside the lock
nonLive.detail: In b107c559's original internal/nakama/watch_room.go, broadcastRoomState RLocks only to collect clientIDs, then RUnlocks and calls SendEventTo(cid, ..., room, true), passing the raw *WatchRoom pointer straight into gorilla's WriteJSON (json.Marshal). Any concurrent JoinRoom/LeaveRoom/SetControl mutating room.Participants (write-locked) while this marshal iterates the same map (unlocked read) is a 'concurrent map read and map write' fatal crash, not just a data race. Confirmed fixed later via room.snapshotLocked() (a locked copy) introduced in e19c8845 (per fable-audit.md section 6, which references watch_room.go:657 from a prior audit pass and 'the prior audit's crash fix (snapshot marshaling)').
nonLive.evidence: internal/nakama/watch_room.go (as introduced in b107c559, function broadcastRoomState); fixed by e19c8845 (current HEAD internal/nakama/watch_room.go:1016-1032 uses room.snapshotLocked())

nonLive.title: 98e9d742 gave the admin a per-user session on networked servers but left the global event plane claiming the admin's real id for a day until 52fcf6c4
nonLive.detail: 98e9d742 (2026-06-22) made every user including the admin get an independent per-user session with its own scoped VideoCore/events on password-protected servers, but a.adminEvents was still built with the real adminID (not the system sentinel) until 52fcf6c4 (2026-06-23) rescoped it. In between, the admin's client would have received duplicate/contending events from both the global adminEvents plane and their new per-user plane, breaking admin playback start (matches the documented 'admin streaming double-claim' known bug). Both commits are in this batch; the fix (52fcf6c4) is the last commit of this pair, so no separate action needed, but noting the self-contained regression window for completeness.

nonLive.title: cdnBackoffDuration only parses numeric Retry-After seconds, silently falls back to exponential backoff for HTTP-date form
nonLive.detail: Retry-After per RFC 7231 can be either delay-seconds or an HTTP-date; cdnBackoffDuration only strconv.Atoi's it, so a CDN sending the date form is silently ignored in favor of the exponential backoff schedule. This is intentional/tested behavior (see the test's own HTTP-date case expecting the 300ms fallback), not a crash, just an unhandled input format worth noting.
nonLive.evidence: internal/directstream/httpstream.go cdnBackoffDuration (~line 55-70); internal/directstream/httpstream_cdn_retry_test.go (HTTP-date test case)

nonLive.title: Echo-rejection heuristic can silently drop a genuine second user's discrete action
nonLive.detail: 87d4b592's crossEcho check drops any discrete action from a different client than lastDiscreteBy within 600ms of the last genuine change, with no way to distinguish a real independent action (two members both pressing pause/seek within the debounce window) from a follower's apply-echo. The dropped action is not surfaced to the sender (no rejection ack), so a legitimately-timed second action is silently swallowed. A later commit (86597516, outside this batch) refines related control-stealing logic for bare pause but does not appear to address this specific cross-user timing collision.
nonLive.evidence: internal/nakama/watch_room.go: echoDebounce = 600*time.Millisecond; crossEcho := room.lastDiscreteBy != "" && senderClientID != room.lastDiscreteBy && time.Since(room.lastDiscreteAt) < echoDebounce (87d4b592)

nonLive.title: Per-token CDN semaphore map (cdnTokenGateInst.sems) grows forever, never evicted

On the always-on Pi deployment this accumulates one small channel per distinct debrid link ever streamed since boot -- bounded in size per-entry but unbounded in count over a long uptime.

nonLive.title: Per-token gate (2 slots) is held for the whole video-serve duration, and nakama rooms share one debrid link across members

Watch-room followers are designed to reuse the driver's shared debrid link (GetUserStreamShare), so a 3rd+ concurrent viewer of the same file would contend for only 2 slots for their entire playback, not just at start. This was left as 'verify via journalctl before a 3-person room test' in the prior audit and nothing in this commit batch resolves it.
nonLive.evidence: internal/directstream/httpstream.go:481-489; internal/directstream/cdngate.go (commit 70d262c0); fable-audit.md:87-99 (§1.4)

nonLive.title: vc_pickSkipCandidate's greedy scoring/tie-break mutates bestScore in a way that's non-obviously correct
nonLive.detail: 19bed7eb's `vc_pickSkipCandidate` picks the chapter closest to 90s, preferring the later one on a near-tie (within 15s), but updates `bestScore = Math.min(score, bestScore)` even on the tie branch where the later (possibly worse-scored) candidate is chosen as `best` — so `bestScore` can silently diverge from the score of the currently selected `best` candidate across iterations. Manually traced examples still produce the intended answer, but the coupling is fragile and order-of-candidates dependent in a way that isn't obviously invariant for all inputs.

nonLive.title: Cached status outranks format quality (resolution/codec/remux) within an audio band — violates decreed quality-over-cache invariant
nonLive.detail: smartCachedPrioritization sorts scoreBand (episode/audio correctness) -> cached -> score, so within the same audio band a cached 480p beats an uncached 1080p REMUX English dub: cache is a band-level key applied before the entire format score, not a tie-break. This is deliberate and test-enshrined — commits a00983fe ('cached-first ranking') and 70f34f49 ('rank by episode -> English dub -> cache -> format'), and autoselect_test.go's 'Cached comes first within the same audio tier' case expects the cached 480p above three uncached 1080p/720p releases. Episode-relevance and audio-language DO sit above cache, so the decree is honored for those two axes only. Flagged high per the audit decree ('cached-first must be a tie-break, never override quality'); user must adjudicate whether format quality was meant to be included, since the code, comments, and tests all encode the opposite.
nonLive.evidence: internal/torrents/autoselect/comparison.go:689-710 (cached compared before score), comparison.go:46-48 (design comment), internal/torrents/autoselect/autoselect_test.go:378-388; commits 70f34f49, a00983fe

Root cause tying findings 1-3 together: the upstream guardPrivileged* helpers (guardPrivilegedLocalExecution, guardPrivilegedExtensionManagement, guardPrivilegedMediastream) were built for the single-user LOCAL-trust model where isTrustedRequest returns true iff there is NO server password; on the fork's networked deployment a password is ALWAYS set, so every one of those guards degrades to allow-all.
